title: Throwing a shallow open bowl
subtitle: A wide, low bowl with a refined foot ring thrown on the wheel
excerpt: A wheel-thrown stoneware bowl opened wide and kept low, with an even wall thickness throughout and a trimmed foot ring. The shallow open form is one of the most demanding throwing shapes because every part of the wall is on display and any unevenness is clearly visible.
sourceNotes: Wheel-throwing technique for shallow bowls derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Compressing and aligning clay on the spinning wheel head until it runs perfectly true with no wobble.
The stage after centring where the floor of the form is pushed down and outward to create the hollow interior.
A clay drying stage where the piece is firm enough to handle and trim without distorting, but still slightly damp.
A trimmed ring on the underside of a thrown pot that raises the base off the surface. The foot ring is cut with a loop tool when the pot is leather-hard.

content.text: A shallow open bowl needs even wall thickness from base to rim. The walls lean outward at 45 degrees or more, so any thick patch stands out in the finished piece. Open wide and low early, then use the wooden rib to shape the curve and compress the clay.

attrs.content: Wear an N95 mask when trimming leather-hard stoneware. Trimming generates fine silica-bearing dust. Fire kilns in a well-ventilated space away from living areas.

content.text: Steps
Centre 500 g of clay and open outward along the floor to the desired bowl width.
Raise the wall in one or two passes and use a rib to shape the curve.
Level the rim with the needle tool and compress it gently.
Run a wire under the bat and move the whole bat to a drying board.
Trim a shallow foot ring when leather-hard.
Centring
Centre

the clay as a wide, flattened dome rather than a tall cone. A low starting profile suits the low finished form: push inward and outward simultaneously during centring to spread the clay as you go. The centred mound should be roughly 150 mm across and 60 mm tall for a 600 g piece.
Opening
Opening

a wide bowl requires a slow, deliberate outward sweep rather than the downward push used for a cylinder. Push both thumbs into the centre, then draw them outward along the floor in a single movement, leaving the floor at an even 8-10 mm thickness. Open to within 20 mm of the intended final rim diameter, leaving room for the wall to thin as it is drawn upward.
Raising and shaping the wall

Pull the wall upward in one or two passes only. A shallow bowl does not benefit from many thin pulls. Instead, use the wooden rib held against the outer wall to compress the curve and refine the angle. Slow the wheel and draw the rib inward and upward in a smooth arc, following the natural curve of the bowl. The rib simultaneously thins the wall and imposes the curve. Finish by compressing the rim gently between thumb and forefinger. Alternatively, fold the chamois over the rim and run it around once.
Removing from the wheel

Use the needle tool to level the rim if needed. Sponge out any standing water from the inside. Draw the wire cutter under the bat in one pass, then lift the entire bat and set it on a drying board. Do not attempt to move a fresh shallow bowl off the bat. The form is too wide and flexible.
Trimming the foot ring
When the bowl is
leather-hard
(firm but still slightly cool to the touch), invert it onto the wheel head. Centre it by tapping gently while the wheel spins slowly. Trim the
foot ring

with the loop tool: cut down the outer edge of the foot first, then across the base, then up the inner edge. The foot ring width should be roughly one-third the diameter of the base. Wear the N95 mask throughout trimming.
Variations
Use the rib at a flatter angle to produce a straight-walled salad bowl shape rather than a curved one.
Facet the outside of the wall while leather-hard using a wire cheese cutter held parallel to the surface.

items.symptom: Rim warps or becomes oval during drying
items.cause: Uneven wall thickness; one side drying faster than the other
items.fix: Cover loosely with plastic sheeting to slow drying for the first 12 hours. Remove from the bat when firm enough to stand without support

items.cause: Clay too wet; opening too fast before walls have any structure
items.fix: Work faster between water applications. Keep the wheel speed up during opening to provide centrifugal support
